rulings.Case Title: INTERVISION SYSTEMS, LLC VS. BEN ESCOBAR ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Evidence Spoliation Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, August 7, 2019, Line 1, PLAINTIFF INTERVISION SYSTEMS, LLC's Motion For Evidence Spoliation Sanctions. Pro Tem Judge Peter Catalanotti, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing Required. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to peter.catalanotti@wilsonelser.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: KAMELIA BEGLEY VS. DELTA DENTAL OF CALIFORNIA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Compel Further Responses And To Produce Documents
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, August 7, 2019, Line 3, PLAINTIFF KAMELIA BEGLEY's Motion To Compel Further Responses And To Produce Documents. Pro Tem Judge Peter Catalanotti, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The Motion is GRANTED and DENIED as follows. First, the Court finds that the Motion as to RFPs 7 and 38 is timely. Defendant's unverified "First Supplemental Response to Plaintiff's Request for Production of Documents and Electronically Stored Information, Set One" was personally served on May 7, 2019. The responses appear on their face to amend RFPs 7 and 38. The verification was served on May 8, 2019 via mail. 50 days after May 8, 2019 is June 27, 2019. The Motion was filed on June 25, 2019, within 45 days of the responses. (Erlich Decl., Ex. I). Second, the Court finds that plaintiff's counsel adequately met and conferred with defense counsel. The Court makes the following specific rulings: 7: The Motion is GRANTED, all objections are overruled. 10: The Motion as to RFP #10 is denied as moot. (See Reply 1:10-12) 18: The Motion is DENIED, defendant provided an adequate response and evidence that all documents have been provided. (Osmani Decl., par. 3-6). 39: The Motion is GRANTED, all objections are overruled. The Court further finds that the documents requested are reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence. The Court finds that the separate statement is sufficiently code-complaint. Amended, verified responses and responsive documents (if applicable) are due within one week of notice of this order. If amended, verified responses and responsive documents (if applicable) are not served within one week of notice of this order, sanctions in the amount of $2,500 are due and payable against defendant and its counsel of record in favor of plaintiff within two weeks of notice of entry of this order. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to peter.catalanotti@wilsonelser.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: ARMANDO REYES VS. STARBUCKS CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Wednesday, August 7, 2019, Line 5, PLAINTIFF ARMANDO REYES'S DEMURRER TO ANSWER TO COMPLAINT. Plaintiff Reyes' demurrer to answer is off calendar. First, the demurrer is untimely under Code of Civil Procedure, Sec. 430.40(b) ["[a] party who has filed a complaint ... may, within 10 days after service of the answer to his pleading, demur to the answer."] Here, defendants served the answer on April 8, 2019 and plaintiff filed the demurrer on May 16, 2019. Second, and more importantly, there is no declaration showing that the parties met and conferred regarding the alleged defects in the answer before this demurrer was filed. (See Code Civ. Proc., Sec. 430.41.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling adversely affects, in whole or in part, any party who is not represented by an attorney, then any other party (whether represented by an attorney or not) may submit on the tentative ruling without appearing for the hearing only if the adversely affected self-represented party has stated in writing or orally that it, he or she will not appear at the hearing. Counsel for Starbucks is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portions of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: RACHEL GOLDBLATT ET AL VS. PRIORITY BICYCLES ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Demurrer To Complaint (Part 2 Of 2 For Purposes Of Entry Of Tentative Ruling Only)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Wednesday, August 7, 2019, Line 6, Demurrer To Complaint (Part 2 Of 2 For Purposes Of Entry Of Tentative Ruling Only). (Continued from Part 1 of 2) Plaintiffs' opposition suggests that they may have additional facts they can plead regarding Velofix Holdings' role in the sale of bikes like the one that allegedly injured Ms. Goldblatt. They are granted leave to amend to add such allegations and to clarify how Velofix Holdings specifically is connected to the bicycle enterprise and how it owed a duty to Ms. Goldblatt. Counsel for defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portions of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. (This is the end of the tentative ruling.) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: RACHEL GOLDBLATT ET AL VS. PRIORITY BICYCLES ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Wednesday, August 7, 2019, Line 6, DEFENDANT VELOFIX HOLDINGS USA, INC.'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT. (Part of 1 of 2) Defendant Velofix Holdings USA, Inc.'s demurrer to Plaintiffs Rachel Goldblatt and Brad Goldblatt's complaint is sustained as to all seven causes of action with 20 days leave to amend. The allegation that Velofix Holdings "partners" with mobile bike workshops such as Defendant Velofix Bay Area is insufficient to indicate where Velofix Holdings fits into the stream of commerce and Plaintiffs' theories of liability. (Compl. Paras. 4-5; see Arriaga v. CitiCapital Commercial Corp. (2008) 167 Cal.App.4th 1527, 1534 [defendant is subject to strict products liability if identifiable as an "integral part of the overall producing and marketing enterprise"] [emphasis added]; Bay Summit Community Assn. v. Shell Oil Co. (1996) 51 Cal.App.4th 762, 776 [products liability claims require "a sufficient causative relationship or connection between the defendant and the product so as to establish" that the defendant received a direct financial benefit from the sale of the product, was integrally involved in the business, and had control over or a substantial ability to influence the manufacturing or distribution process].) The complaint contains no other allegations specific to Velofix Holdings, only generic allegations against Defendants collectively. (See Hawkins v. TAC Int'l Airlines, S.A. (2014) 223 Cal.App.4th 466, 478-479 [boilerplate allegations are insufficient to survive demurrer]; Doheny Park Terrace Homeowners Assn., Inc. v. Truck Ins. Exchange (2005) 132 Cal.App.4th 1076, 1099 [complaint must make more than generic allegations that fail to set forth the essential facts with sufficient particularity to acquaint each defendant with "the nature, source and extent of [each] cause of action"].) By contrast, the alleged role of each of the other defendants is made clearer. (See Compl. Paras. 5-6, 12 [alleging that Velofix Bay Area "sells, repair and services bicycles" in San Francisco; Defendant Bay Area Bike Services, LLC provides "on-site repair"; and Defendant Priority Bicycles "manufactured, distributed, and sold" the bike Ms. Goldblatt rode when she was injured].) While pleading in the alternative is generally acceptable, Plaintiffs have not sufficiently alleged how Velofix Holdings is alleged to be connected to the bicycle enterprise and Ms. Goldblatt's injury for purposes of strict liability. Furthermore, this is not a situation involving fraudulent concealment that justifies a less specific form of pleading as was the case in Turner v. Milstein (1951) 103 Cal.App.2d 651, 658. In addition, without more clarity on the role of Velofix Holdings, the negligence claims are not properly supported as it is unclear what duty was breached. (See Minnegren v. Nozar (2016) 4 Cal.App.5th 500, 507 [negligence claims require allegations that support the conclusion that the defendant breached a duty of care to the plaintiff].) (Tentative Ruling Continues in Part 2 of 2) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: ELLA RATMANSKY VS. JEWISH HOME AND SENIOR LIVING FOUNDATION ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Wednesday, August 7, 2019, Line 7, DEFENDANT ELAINE GECHT'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT. (Part 1 of 2) Defendant Elaine Gecht, M.D.'s demurrer to plaintiff Ella Ratmansky's complaint is sustained without leave to amend to the first cause of action for professional negligence and the second cause of action for willful misconduct, and is sustained with 20 days leave to amend as to the third cause of action for elder abuse. The professional negligence cause of action against Dr. Gecht is time-barred under Code of Civil Procedure § 340.5, as plaintiff concedes. So is the second cause of action for willful misconduct, because the gravamen of that cause of action is also a claim for personal injuries based on professional negligence. (Carter v. Prime Healthcare Paradise Valley LLC (2011) 198 Cal.App.4th 396, 412, 413 [in case alleging that plaintiffs' decedent suffered serious personal injury and emotional distress as a result of hospital's failures to treat and document his pressure ulcers properly, "although labeled 'willful misconduct,' plaintiff's third cause of action is really one for personal injuries to Grant based on the Hospital's alleged professional negligence and is barred by the statute of limitations"].) The complaint alleges that during the period of plaintiff's decedent's residence at the Jewish Home, she was under the care of Dr. Gecht, who acted as her primary care physician. (Compl. Paras. 9, 19, 23.) It further alleges that Defendants, despite their knowledge that decedent was confined to a wheelchair and was at high risk for developing a skin condition, did not turn or reposition her, even at night, resulting in her developing serious pressure ulcers or bedsores and severe pain. (Compl. Paras. 14-20.) Those allegations are sufficient to allege that Dr. Gecht was a person "having the care or custody of an elder or a dependent adult" within the meaning of the Elder Abuse Act and Dependent Adult Civil Protection Act. (Welf. & Inst. Code § 15610.57(a)(1); see Winn v. Pioneer Medical Group, Inc. (2016) 63 Cal.4th 148, 153 [Act applies if the defendant health care provider "had a substantial caretaking or custodial relationship, involving ongoing responsibility for one or more basic needs, with the elder patient"; "An individual might assume the responsibility for attending to an elder's basic needs in a variety of contexts and locations, including beyond the confines of a residential care facility."] [emphasis added].) Certainly, a physician with primary responsibility to provide medical care for an extremely elderly patient in a residential nursing home who is non-ambulatory and unable to sleep in a bed engages in far "more than casual or limited interactions," as might be the case of a physician who provides episodic care to a patient on an outpatient basis. (Id. at 158.) (Tentative Ruling Continues in Part 2 of 2) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: ELLA RATMANSKY VS. JEWISH HOME AND SENIOR LIVING FOUNDATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Demurrer To Complaint (Part 2 Of 2 For Purposes Of Entry Of Tentative Ruling Only)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Wednesday, August 7, 2019, Line 7, Demurrer To Complaint (Part 2 Of 2 For Purposes Of Entry Of Tentative Ruling Only) (Continued from Part 1 of 2) These allegations are adequate to plead meet the "physical abuse . . . , or neglect" element of a cause of action under the Act. (See Carter v. Prime Healthcare Paradise Valley LLC, 198 Cal.App.4th at 406-407 [elements of cause of action include that the defendant (1) had responsibility for meeting the basic needs of the elder, such as medical care; (2) knew of conditions that made the elder unable to provide for her own basic needs; and (3) denied or withheld services necessary to meet the elder's basic needs, either with knowledge that injury was substantially certain to result or with conscious disregard of the high probability of such injury].) However, they do not appear to be sufficient to allege "that the defendant has been guilty of recklessness, oppression, fraud, or malice in the commission of this abuse." (Welf. & Inst. Code § 15657; see, e.g., Cochrum v. Costa Victoria Healthcare, LLC (2018) 25 Cal.App.5th 1034, 1044-1050 [trial court did not err in granting JNOV where there was no substantial evidence of recklessness on part of nursing facility and operator]; compare Fenimore v. Regents of the University of California (2016) 245 Cal.App.4th 1339, 1347-1351 [trial court erred in sustaining demurrer without leave to amend where amended complaint stated at least one viable theory of elder abuse based on recklessness].) Because plaintiff indicates in her opposition that since filing her complaint, she has discovered additional facts supporting her elder abuse cause of action, she is granted 20 days leave to amend that cause of action. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Dr. Gecht is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portions of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. (This is the end of the tentative ruling) =(302/EPS)
